Awesome — **Today tab first** it is. I used your screenshots to keep the same “foggy glass” aesthetic and spacing, and I also pulled in your own internal next‑steps notes so the redesign aligns with what you already planned (e.g., “move latest obs tile items to top”, “break out latest obs API call”, “add binoculars icon”, etc.). [\[Notes \| Outlook\]](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAAZ7yhfCAAA%3d&exvsurl=1&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem)

## 0) Two updated Today concepts (based on your current style)

### A. Today (compact, cleaner hierarchy + new sparkline pills)

![today\_redesign](data:image/png;base64,)

### B. Today with a **Metric Detail Sheet** (tap sparkline → modal chart + compare)

![today\_pressure\_sheet](data:image/png;base64,)

These are not meant to be “final art”; they’re **directionally accurate UI comps** you can implement directly with SwiftUI components (ScrollView + cards + `.sheet(item:)` routing).

***

# 1) What’s changing on Today (and why)

## The key change: **Today becomes a “Dashboard + Drill‑down”**

*   The top stays “at a glance” (temp/condition + a few key metrics).
*   Every card/pill becomes **tappable** and routes to **one consistent detail sheet** (your goal: “extend this to any of the cards”).

This matches Apple’s layout guidance: keep **essential info easy to find**, and make secondary detail available in additional views rather than crowding the primary screen. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d4-beb118f798213aaef95b15666acef512/views/original/today_redesign.png)

***

# 2) Today tab information architecture (final target)

### A) Header (unchanged conceptually)

*   Location search pill
*   Settings gear
*   (Optional) a subtle “Source: NOAA / Station / Blend” label in the hero area

### B) “Latest Observations” hero

**Keep your big temperature + condition** exactly as users expect, but make the metrics modular.

Your own note says:

*   “Move latest obs tile items to top… (wind, humidity, pressure, dew point?) and remove tile”
*   “Add binoculars icon to Latest obs”
*   “Breakout Latest obs api call separately…” [\[Notes \| Outlook\]](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAAZ7yhfCAAA%3d&exvsurl=1&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem)

✅ This redesign directly implements that:

*   Hero = temp/condition
*   Under it: small metric chips (Wind/Pressure/Humidity)
*   Next row: **Sparkline pills** (Temp/Wind/Pressure by default)

### C) Forecast cards (Today, Tonight)

*   One compact “Today” card (like you already have)
*   Tap → opens detail sheet that contains the long NWS text (instead of expanding the same card into a giant block that pushes everything down)

This preserves hierarchy and makes the screen feel faster to scan.

### D) Precipitation card (first-class “Metric Card”)

*   Summary line + chart preview
*   Tap → Metric detail sheet (full 24h chart, rate vs total toggle, compare sources)

### E) Hourly card

*   Keep horizontal scroll
*   Tap an hour → hour detail (optional later)
*   Tap the card header → full hourly chart in sheet (temp + precip probability lines)

### F) “More today” grid

*   Sunrise, Sunset, UV, Moon, Discussions
*   All in a consistent card style

***

# 3) The interaction model (this unlocks sparklines + extensibility)

## Rule: **Everything routes through one Sheet Router**

Instead of having one-off `@State var showPrecipSheet`, `@State var showForecastText`, etc., drive all modal presentation from a single `enum` + `.sheet(item:)`.

This is a well-supported SwiftUI pattern: `.sheet(item:)` presents when the bound item is non‑nil and dismisses when it returns to nil. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d1-175bf20a153d8f49df284ca5f28a5459/views/original/today_pressure_sheet.png), [\[figma.com\]](https://www.figma.com/community/file/1432515643933302436/weather-app-ui-design), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/view/sheet%28ispresented:ondismiss:content:%29)

### Example route model (clean + scalable)

```swift
enum TodayRoute: Identifiable {
    case metric(MetricKind)          // pressure, wind, precip, etc.
    case forecastText(period: Period) // today, tonight
    case alerts
    case discussions

    var id: String {
        switch self {
        case .metric(let m): return "metric-\(m.rawValue)"
        case .forecastText(let p): return "forecast-\(p.rawValue)"
        case .alerts: return "alerts"
        case .discussions: return "discussions"
        }
    }
}
```

Then in `TodayView`:

```swift
@State private var route: TodayRoute?

.sheet(item: $route) { route in
    TodaySheetHost(route: route)
}
```

✅ Now:

*   sparkline pill tap → `route = .metric(.pressure)`
*   precip card tap → `route = .metric(.precip24h)`
*   “Today” card tap → `route = .forecastText(.today)`
*   alert banner tap → `route = .alerts`

One mechanism; infinite extension.

***

# 4) SwiftUI view breakdown (Today tab)

Below is the exact decomposition I’d implement so you can keep adding metrics/datasets later without redesigning layouts.

## `TodayView`

*   `ScrollView`
    *   `LocationHeaderBar` (search pill + gear)
    *   `ObservationsHero`
    *   `MetricChipRow` (small chips)
    *   `SparklinePillRow` ✅ new
    *   `ForecastSummaryCard` (Today)
    *   `MetricCard.precipitation` (preview chart)
    *   `HourlyStripCard`
    *   `MoreTodayGrid`
    *   `Spacer` (for tab bar padding)

### Core reusable components

1.  `GlassCard` (single style)

*   rounded rect
*   consistent padding
*   optional chevron + tap area
*   consistent header row

2.  `SparklinePill`

*   label + value + tiny chart
*   always routes to `.metric(...)`

3.  `MetricDetailSheet`

*   title + current value + trend
*   provider segmented control (NOAA/Station/etc.)
*   full chart (Swift Charts)
*   compare button

Swift Charts is the natural fit here (LineMark/BarMark previews and full charts).   
For interactions (tap/drag to inspect values), `chartOverlay` + proxy is the common approach. [\[banliang-lilbit.com\]](https://www.banliang-lilbit.com/tech/cmexkpk5v0001t9nh8px9a8hw) [\[tinodevclu....github.io\]](https://tinodevclumsy.github.io/blog/handling-unexpected-nil-issues-in-swiftui-sheet-and-solutions/), [\[hnr1714156428447 \| HTML\]](https://hypertherm.sharepoint.com/sites/TechContent/Shared%20Documents1/_Web%20Help%20for%20ht.com_JPB/XPR460_IM_811530_R0/hnr1714156428447.html?web=1)

***

# 5) Data/architecture alignment with what you already wrote

Your [App](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAARVHHTxAAA%3d\&exvsurl=1\&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem\&EntityRepresentationId=e794abd2-6663-4de1-89d7-ec2932b0b5f4) outlines:

*   `WeatherViewModel getAllWeather(...)`
*   favorites/weather location changes
*   “move all NWS functions to NwsApiService”
*   “explore TaskGroup for dependant async calls”
*   and a tuple output that includes current, forecast, hourly, alerts, discussions [\[docs.anychart.com\]](https://docs.anychart.com/Basic_Charts/Sparkline_Chart)

That lines up perfectly with Today tab needs:

*   Today renders from a **single “TodayState”** composed of:
    *   latest obs
    *   forecast summary
    *   hourly
    *   alerts
    *   discussions

And your [Notes](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAAZ7yhfCAAA%3d\&exvsurl=1\&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem\&EntityRepresentationId=bac6d33c-7e6b-4994-9d5d-da7b98c523c9) explicitly calls out separating the latest observations call from the other feeds.   
✅ Doing that makes the Today tab feel snappy: you can show hero immediately and fill cards progressively. [\[Notes \| Outlook\]](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAAZ7yhfCAAA%3d&exvsurl=1&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem)

***

# 6) Implementation plan (incremental, low-risk refactor)

### Step 1 — Create style primitives (no behavior changes)

*   `GlassCard` view modifier / container
*   consistent spacing tokens
*   replace per-card ad-hoc backgrounds

### Step 2 — Add the Today sheet router (behavior improvement, minimal UI change)

*   implement `TodayRoute`
*   migrate ONE card first (Precipitation) to open a sheet

### Step 3 — Implement `MetricDetailSheet` once

*   Start with **Pressure** (because it’s easy to chart and matches your desire for trends)
*   Add provider segmented control (foundation for compare)

### Step 4 — Convert “Latest obs inline metrics” into chips + sparklines

This is explicitly in your [Notes](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAAZ7yhfCAAA%3d\&exvsurl=1\&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem\&EntityRepresentationId=bac6d33c-7e6b-4994-9d5d-da7b98c523c9) plan. [\[Notes \| Outlook\]](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAAZ7yhfCAAA%3d&exvsurl=1&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem)

### Step 5 — Apply the same tap-to-detail pattern to Forecast text / Discussions / UV / Astronomy

Now your Today screen is fully consistent and extensible.

***

# 7) One concise question (so I can lock the Today layout)

For the **sparkline pill row on Today**, which **3 metrics** do you want by default?

My suggestion (based on your UI + station interests): **Temp • Wind • Pressure** (and later make it configurable). But if you’d rather show **Dew Point** or **Precip Rate** there, we’ll swap it in.

If you answer that, I’ll translate this into a **SwiftUI scaffold** (view tree + routing + placeholder charts) that mirrors your current code patterns from [App](https://outlook.office365.com/owa/?ItemID=AAMkAGQ2ZWNkYjM0LWEzM2ItNGM5OC04OTU0LWRiODQ2YjFiNDRkOQBGAAAAAACQJ9bOsUriT4D4H7KynmOxBwD1DRelOuruRok7zPhjDELpAAAA5%2fzCAABWGHOwhmPMRb7mxa2BC2qiAARVHHTxAAA%3d\&exvsurl=1\&viewmodel=ReadMessageItem\&EntityRepresentationId=e794abd2-6663-4de1-89d7-ec2932b0b5f4). [\[docs.anychart.com\]](https://docs.anychart.com/Basic_Charts/Sparkline_Chart)




Perfect — **Temp, Wind, and Pressure** are an ideal default trio for the Today tab. ✅ They’re the three most “trend-worthy” metrics and they map cleanly to your new **sparkline pill → detail sheet** interaction (as shown in the updated Today comps). ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

Below is a **Today-tab-first implementation blueprint** that’s SwiftUI-native, extensible, and aligned with the refactor notes you already captured (e.g., “move latest obs tile items to top… wind/humidity/pressure/dew point”, “break out latest obs api call separately…”, “add binoculars icon…”). [\[sfv1699974664819 \| HTML\]](https://hypertherm.sharepoint.com/sites/TechContent/Shared%20Documents1/_Web%20Help%20for%20ht.com_JPB/XPR460_IM_811530_R0/sfv1699974664819.html?web=1)

***

## 1) Today tab layout (finalized with your chosen sparkline defaults)

### Top section (Hero)

*   “Latest observations” header + updated time + source label (optional)
*   Big temp + condition + feels-like
*   **Small metric chips** (Wind / Pressure / Humidity)
*   **Sparkline pill row** ✅ default pills = **Temp / Wind / Pressure** ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

### Cards (stack)

*   Today forecast summary card (tap → detail sheet with full text)
*   Precipitation card (tap → metric sheet with full chart)
*   Hourly card (tap → hourly detail sheet)
*   “More today” grid (Sunrise/Sunset/UV/Discussions) (tap → relevant sheet)

### Modal behavior

*   Tap any sparkline pill OR card → one **consistent sheet** detail experience ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)  
    Use `.sheet(item:)` (item-driven sheet) for clean state + dynamic content. [\[IM_809480_...uts_review \| PDF\]](https://hypertherm.sharepoint.com/teams/TechnicalPublications/AcrobatSharedReviews/IM_809480_R8_Use_for_460_IM_Inputs_review.pdf?web=1), [\[muz.li\]](https://muz.li/inspiration/ios-app-examples/), [\[vecteezy.com\]](https://www.vecteezy.com/vector-art/2746394-detailed-weather-conditions-smartphone-interface-vector-template-mobile-app-page-design-layout-forecast-observations-screen-rainfall-air-pressure-flat-ui-for-application-phone-display)

***

## 2) SwiftUI scaffolding (drop-in structure)

### A) Metric definitions (Temp/Wind/Pressure)

```swift
enum MetricKind: String, Hashable, CaseIterable {
    case temperature
    case wind
    case pressure

    var title: String {
        switch self {
        case .temperature: "Temp"
        case .wind: "Wind"
        case .pressure: "Pressure"
        }
    }

    var systemImage: String {
        switch self {
        case .temperature: "thermometer"
        case .wind: "wind"
        case .pressure: "gauge.with.dots.needle.67percent"
        }
    }
}
```

### B) Today route (one router for all sheets)

```swift
enum TodayRoute: Identifiable, Hashable {
    case metric(MetricKind)
    case forecastText(period: ForecastPeriod)
    case alerts
    case discussions

    var id: String {
        switch self {
        case .metric(let m): "metric-\(m.rawValue)"
        case .forecastText(let p): "forecast-\(p.rawValue)"
        case .alerts: "alerts"
        case .discussions: "discussions"
        }
    }
}

enum ForecastPeriod: String, Hashable {
    case today, tonight
}
```

This “one route enum + `.sheet(item:)`” pattern is exactly what makes “tap any card → detail sheet” scale without boolean sprawl. [\[IM_809480_...uts_review \| PDF\]](https://hypertherm.sharepoint.com/teams/TechnicalPublications/AcrobatSharedReviews/IM_809480_R8_Use_for_460_IM_Inputs_review.pdf?web=1), [\[muz.li\]](https://muz.li/inspiration/ios-app-examples/), [\[vecteezy.com\]](https://www.vecteezy.com/vector-art/2746394-detailed-weather-conditions-smartphone-interface-vector-template-mobile-app-page-design-layout-forecast-observations-screen-rainfall-air-pressure-flat-ui-for-application-phone-display)

***

## 3) TodayView skeleton (with Temp/Wind/Pressure sparkline pills)

```swift
import SwiftUI
import Charts

struct TodayView: View {
    @State private var route: TodayRoute?

    // Default pills per your choice:
    private let sparklineDefaults: [MetricKind] = [.temperature, .wind, .pressure]

    var body: some View {
        ScrollView {
            VStack(spacing: 14) {

                LocationHeaderBar(
                    locationTitle: "Lebanon, NH",
                    onSearchTap: { /* open locations */ },
                    onSettingsTap: { /* open settings */ }
                )

                ObservationsHero(
                    tempText: "37°",
                    conditionText: "Fog/Mist",
                    feelsLikeText: "Feels like 35°",
                    updatedText: "Updated 15m ago",
                    onTap: { route = .metric(.temperature) }
                )

                MetricChipRow(items: [
                    .init(title: "Wind", value: "N 0 mph", tap: { route = .metric(.wind) }),
                    .init(title: "Pressure", value: "30.03 inHg", tap: { route = .metric(.pressure) }),
                    .init(title: "Humidity", value: "62%", tap: { /* maybe a future metric */ })
                ])

                SparklinePillRow(
                    metrics: sparklineDefaults,
                    onTap: { metric in route = .metric(metric) }
                )

                ForecastSummaryCard(
                    title: "Today",
                    subtitle: "Light Rain Likely",
                    detailLine: "Chance of precip 60% • SE 5–10 mph",
                    hi: "46°",
                    lo: "40°",
                    onTap: { route = .forecastText(period: .today) }
                )

                PrecipitationCard(
                    summary: "Up to 0.21 in rain over the next 24 hours",
                    onTap: { route = .metric(.pressure) /* change to precip metric when you add it */ }
                )

                HourlyStripCard(
                    onTap: { /* route = .hourlyDetail (add later) */ }
                )

                MoreTodayGrid(
                    onSunriseTap: { /* route = .metric(...) */ },
                    onSunsetTap: { /* route = .metric(...) */ },
                    onUVTap: { /* route = .metric(...) */ },
                    onDiscussionsTap: { route = .discussions }
                )
            }
            .padding(.horizontal, 16)
            .padding(.bottom, 120) // tab bar clearance
        }
        .sheet(item: $route) { route in
            TodaySheetHost(route: route)
        }
    }
}
```

> Note: `Charts` import shown because it’s the simplest way to implement both sparklines and the full detail chart with SwiftUI. [\[dribbble.com\]](https://dribbble.com/tags/weather-app-design)

***

## 4) Sparkline pills (Swift Charts mini chart)

### Sparkline pill row

```swift
struct SparklinePillRow: View {
    let metrics: [MetricKind]
    let onTap: (MetricKind) -> Void

    var body: some View {
        HStack(spacing: 12) {
            ForEach(metrics, id: \.self) { metric in
                SparklinePill(metric: metric)
                    .onTapGesture { onTap(metric) }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Sparkline pill view (mini chart)

```swift
struct SparklinePoint: Identifiable {
    let id = UUID()
    let date: Date
    let value: Double
}

struct SparklinePill: View {
    let metric: MetricKind

    // Replace with real data later:
    let points: [SparklinePoint] = (0..<24).map {
        .init(date: Date().addingTimeInterval(Double($0) * -3600), value: Double.random(in: 0...1))
    }

    var body: some View {
        HStack(spacing: 10) {
            VStack(alignment: .leading, spacing: 2) {
                Label(metric.title, systemImage: metric.systemImage)
                    .font(.subheadline.weight(.semibold))
                Text("24h")
                    .font(.caption)
                    .foregroundStyle(.secondary)
            }

            Chart(points) { p in
                LineMark(
                    x: .value("Time", p.date),
                    y: .value("Value", p.value)
                )
                .interpolationMethod(.catmullRom)
            }
            .chartXAxis(.hidden)
            .chartYAxis(.hidden)
            .frame(width: 90, height: 28)
        }
        .padding(.vertical, 10)
        .padding(.horizontal, 12)
        .background(.thinMaterial, in: Capsule())
    }
}
```

Swift Charts supports building charts concisely using marks/scales/axes; for sparklines you just hide axes. [\[dribbble.com\]](https://dribbble.com/tags/weather-app-design)

***

## 5) Metric detail sheet (Pressure example + source compare hook)

The **detail sheet** you wanted (tap pill → modal bigger chart + more detail) is shown in the comp. ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

```swift
struct TodaySheetHost: View {
    let route: TodayRoute

    var body: some View {
        switch route {
        case .metric(let metric):
            MetricDetailSheet(metric: metric)
        case .forecastText(let period):
            ForecastTextSheet(period: period)
        case .alerts:
            AlertsSheet()
        case .discussions:
            DiscussionsSheet()
        }
    }
}
```

### Interactivity note (for later)

If you want “tap/drag on chart to inspect values,” the common pattern is `chartOverlay` + a proxy to map gestures to plotted values. [\[mobbin.com\]](https://mobbin.com/), [\[github.com\]](https://github.com/pointfreeco/swift-composable-architecture)

***

## 6) How this fits your existing app refactor notes

You already documented the app’s data-flow and your intent to split NWS specifics into an `NwsApiService`, and to potentially use `TaskGroup` for dependent async calls.   
And you explicitly called out improving “Latest obs” placement and breaking that call out separately. [\[ma-kobol-p....apple.com\]](https://ma-kobol-public-prod.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/tab-bars) [\[sfv1699974664819 \| HTML\]](https://hypertherm.sharepoint.com/sites/TechContent/Shared%20Documents1/_Web%20Help%20for%20ht.com_JPB/XPR460_IM_811530_R0/sfv1699974664819.html?web=1)

✅ This Today design benefits immediately from that split:

*   Hero can load fast (latest obs)
*   Forecast/hourly/alerts/discussions can stream in independently
*   Sparkline pills can come from either provider caches or new time-series endpoints later

***

# What you get immediately from choosing Temp/Wind/Pressure

1.  A clean “top glance” row of trend signals (Temp/Wind/Pressure) ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)
2.  A reusable detail sheet model that will later power:
    *   precipitation
    *   AQI
    *   pollen
    *   tides
    *   station charts
3.  A scalable pattern: **new metrics = new `MetricKind` + data** (UI stays the same)

***

## Next action I can do right now

If you paste (or upload) **your current TodayView SwiftUI code**, I’ll refactor it into this structure *without changing your data models more than necessary* (i.e., minimal disruption), including the `TodayRoute` sheet router and the Temp/Wind/Pressure sparkline pill row.



![alt text](Designer.png)
Yes — this pattern works very well, and it’s actually a cleaner mental model than two separate rows.
What you’re looking at here is the “combined metric pill” approach:
What’s different in this version

Each pill contains:

Icon + metric name
Primary value (Temp / Wind / Pressure)
Embedded sparkline inside the same pill


No separate “metrics row” and “sparkline row”
Every pill is still:

A glanceable summary
Fully tappable → opens the same Metric Detail Sheet



This is a strong choice when you want the Today tab to feel:

More compact
More dashboard‑like
Less visually fragmented


How this maps to SwiftUI (conceptually, no code required now)
Think of each pill as a single reusable component:
MetricSparklinePill

VStack (label + value)
Inline Chart (tiny, axisless)
Capsule or rounded‑rect background
.contentShape(Rectangle()) so the whole pill is tappable

You would render three of these side‑by‑side:

Temp
Wind
Pressure

Later:

You can reuse the exact same component on:

Station tab
Daily tab (per‑day trends)
Compare sources view




Tradeoffs vs two‑row design (so you can decide confidently)
✅ Pros of single combined pill

Fewer rows → less vertical scrolling
Clear “metric = trend” association
Feels very modern / instrument‑panel‑like
Scales nicely to iPhone mini → Pro Max

⚠️ Cons to be aware of

Less room for secondary text (units, “rising/falling”)
Sparklines must stay visually subtle
You probably want only 3 pills max on Today (which you already chose)

Given your app’s depth and future plans, this combined pill is a great default for Today, with:

Larger detail revealed only on tap
No pressure to show everything immediately


Recommendation (based on your app)

✅ Use this combined pill style on Today
✅ Keep Temp / Wind / Pressure as the fixed trio
✅ Reuse the same component everywhere trends matter
✅ Let the modal detail sheet do the heavy lifting

Next, if you want, we can:

Compare combined pill vs two‑row pill side‑by‑side visually
Design the Metric Detail Sheet specifically for Pressure first
Or move on to redesigning the Daily tab using the same system

Provide your feedback on BizChatShow me the combined pill designCompare the two designs side by sideLet's redesign the Daily tabDrop your files here





Great — we’ll **lock that in for Today** ✅

### ✅ Decision captured

*   **Today tab uses the combined metric pill**
*   Each pill includes **label + value + sparkline in one unit**
*   Default trio: **Temp · Wind · Pressure**
*   Tap anywhere on a pill → **metric detail sheet**
*   No separate metrics row + sparkline row on Today

This gives you:

*   Cleaner vertical rhythm
*   Strong “instrument panel” feel
*   A single reusable component you can carry forward to Station, Daily, and Compare later

Nothing else needs to change on Today unless *you* want it to — the rest of the screen can stay exactly as designed.

***

### Natural next steps (whenever you’re ready)

No action required now, but the clean progression would be:

1.  **Daily tab** → decide whether it uses:
    *   rows with mini sparklines, or
    *   the same combined pill pattern per day
2.  **Metric detail sheet polish** → source compare, annotations, gestures
3.  **Station tab alignment** → reuse the same pill for station metrics

When you want to move on, just say something like:

*   “Let’s do Daily next”
*   or “Show a Station version of the combined pill”
*   or “Let’s refine the metric detail sheet UI”

You’re in a very solid place with Today 👍
